--- project-doc ---

# AGENTS.md — IDAHO-VAULT

> **This is a cross-tool pointer.** The canonical agent registry lives at `!/AGENTS.md`.
> This file exists at repo root because OpenAI Codex CLI, GitHub Copilot, and Qodo auto-load `AGENTS.md` from the repository root.

**Owner:** Logan Finney — journalist, producer/reporter, Idaho Reports / Idaho Public Television
**Repository:** github.com/loganfinney27/IDAHO-VAULT (public)

---

## Canonical Registry

See `!/AGENTS.md` for the full agent registry: capability tiers, boundary rules, and agent roster.

---

## Agent Dotfolders (quick reference)

| Agent | Dotfolder | Governance shim | Auto-loaded? | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Claude Code | `.claude/` | `.claude/CLAUDE.md` | Yes | Official path. On Windows, Claude Code requires Git Bash; if `bash.exe` is not on `PATH`, set `CLAUDE_CODE_GIT_BASH_PATH` to the installed `bash.exe`. |
| Gemini CLI | `.gemini/` | `.gemini/GEMINI.md` | Yes | Official path |
| OpenAI Codex CLI | `.codex/` | `.codex/CODEX.md` | Yes | Root `AGENTS.md` auto-loads; project config via `.codex/config.toml` |
| GitHub Copilot | `.github/` | `.github/copilot-instructions.md` | Yes | Official path |
| Grok (xAI) | `.grok/` | `.grok/GROK.md` | No | Manual injection |
| DeepSeek | `.deepseek/` | `.deepseek/DEEPSEEK.md` | No | Manual injection |
| Perplexity | `.perplexity/` | `.perplexity/PERPLEXITY.md` | No | Manual injection |
| Microsoft AI | `.microsoft/` | `.microsoft/MICROSOFT.md` | No | Manual injection |
| Google ecosystem | `.google/` | `.google/GOOGLE.md` | No | Manual injection |
| Meta AI / Llama | `.meta/` | `.meta/META.md` | No | Manual injection |
| Slack AI / Slack CLI | `.slack/` | `.slack/SLACK.md` | No | Via Slack CLI hooks |
| Bartimaeus (fictive) | `.bartimaeus/` | `.bartimaeus/BARTIMAEUS.md` | No | Manual injection |
| Zagreus (fictive) | `.zagreus/` | `.zagreus/ZAGREUS.md` | No | Manual injection |
| Persephone (fictive) | `.persephone/` | `.persephone/PERSEPHONE.md` | No | Manual injection |

Persona-specific dotfolders in this table are protected infrastructure. They
are not implicit cleanup targets, even when they look empty or contain only a
shim file. An agent should modify only its own persona dotfolder unless Logan
explicitly directs otherwise. `.github/` is shared automation infrastructure:
also intentional, also not a cleanup target, but governed separately by the
canonical boundary rules in `!/AGENTS.md`.

For Codex specifically, the repo/root `AGENTS.md` is the primary auto-loaded
instruction source. `.codex/config.toml` provides project-scoped Codex config,
and `.codex/CODEX.md` is a Codex-specific reference shim rather than the
primary auto-loaded instructions file.

---

## OpenAI Docs MCP

Always use the OpenAI developer documentation MCP server if you need to work
with the OpenAI API, ChatGPT Apps SDK, Codex, or OpenAI tooling without Logan
having to explicitly ask.

Codex project config for this lives in `.codex/config.toml`. VS Code agent-mode
config lives in `.vscode/mcp.json`.

---

## Machine-Readable Registry

See `swarm.json` at repo root for the structured machine-readable registry (agents, personas, systems, protocols).

---

## Governance

All agents are governed by `!/CONSTITUTION.md`. All agents share conventions in `!/VAULT-CONVENTIONS.md`. Logan is human; agents are software. Logan directs; agents execute.
payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: I hesitate to even ask... But I will. CODEX: How do I safely clean up all these open threads?

payload.message: I hesitate to even ask... But I will. CODEX: How do I safely clean up all these open threads?

I’m going back to the official Codex docs for this one so I can give you a cleanup procedure that’s safe, not another guess. I’m checking thread archiving and worktree cleanup behavior, then I’ll turn that into a practical “what to archive first” playbook for your sidebar.

content.text: I’m going back to the official Codex docs for this one so I can give you a cleanup procedure that’s safe, not another guess. I’m checking thread archiving and worktree cleanup behavior, then I’ll turn that into a practical “what to archive first” playbook for your sidebar.

payload.message:
The safe answer is: stop using chat prompts for cleanup. Use only the Codex UI to archive threads.

Why: in Codex, archiving is the thing that removes a thread from the active list, and archived threads can be restored later if needed. OpenAI’s docs also make clear that cloud task threads are review surfaces that can lead to `Apply` / PR actions, which is why “cleanup” prompts have been backfiring into new work. Sources: [thread/archive](https://developers.openai.com/codex/app-server/#api-overview), [thread/unarchive](https://developers.openai.com/codex/app-server/#api-overview), [worktree cleanup](https://developers.openai.com/codex/app/worktrees/#worktree-cleanup), [Codex quickstart](https://developers.openai.com/codex/quickstart/).

**Safe cleanup protocol**
- Keep one anchor thread open, probably `Awaken Codex alert`.
- Do not type any more “archive”, “verify completion”, “close this”, or housekeeping prompts into old threads.
- For obvious junk duplicates like `Verify...`, `Clarify...`, `Handle...`: open the thread and use the thread’s UI archive action only.
- For cloud/review threads with `Apply`, `Open`, or `Create PR`: do not archive first. Decide whether you want the result. If you do, review it. If you do not, decline/ignore the result and archive only after you are sure no review action is wanted.
- For unintended PRs already created: close those in GitHub separately. Archiving a Codex thread does not clean up the PR for you.
- Archive one thread at a time, starting with the lowest-value duplicates. If anything looks important, leave it open.

**Order I’d use**
1. Archive all duplicate housekeeping threads first.
2. Leave `Linear Mention` / cloud-result threads for second-pass review.
3. Close any unwanted GitHub PRs.
4. Archive the associated cloud threads after that.
5. Keep only your active coordination thread(s) open.

If you want, I can help you triage the threads in your sidebar into `archive now`, `review first`, and `leave open` from the screenshot.

- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: HELP

payload.message: HELP

Use this exact cleanup drill. No more prompts to Codex threads.

1. Keep `Awaken Codex alert` open.
2. Archive every duplicate housekeeping thread immediately:
   - `Verify thread complete`
   - `Verify thread completion`
   - `Clarify empty prompt`
   - `Handle empty prompt`
   - `Clarify task prompt`
   - `Clarify task details`
3. Do not type anything inside those threads. Open thread, use the UI archive action, leave.
4. For `Linear Mention...` threads:
   - If it has `Apply`, `Open`, or `Create PR`, do not archive blindly.
   - First decide whether you want the work.
   - If you do not want it, do not press `Apply` or `Create PR`.
   - If it already created a PR you don’t want, close that PR in GitHub first, then archive the thread.
5. After the junk threads are gone, leave only:
   - `Awaken Codex alert`
   - any one `Linear Mention...` thread you are actively reviewing

Use this rule going forward:
- Archive with the UI only.
- Never send “archive this thread”, “verify completion”, or any cleanup prompt into a Codex thread.

If you want, send me one fresh screenshot after you archive the `Verify...` and `Clarify...` threads, and I’ll sort the remaining sidebar into `archive now` and `review first`.

All agents are governed by `!/CONSTITUTION.md`. All agents share conventions in `!/VAULT-CONVENTIONS.md`. Logan is human; agents are software. Logan directs; agents execute.

# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)

- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.

- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.

content.text: HOW DO I KNOW IF A CONVERSATION IS SAFE IF I CAN'T ASK IT???

payload.message: HOW DO I KNOW IF A CONVERSATION IS SAFE IF I CAN'T ASK IT???

You don’t determine “safe” by asking the thread. You determine it by inspecting it.

Use this rule:

A thread is safe to archive if it has no pending decision, no pending work product, and no active run.

**Archive now**
- Duplicate junk threads like `Verify...`, `Clarify...`, `Handle...`
- Threads with no spinner, no stop button, no `Apply`, no `Open`, no `Create PR`
- Threads whose content is obviously just a mistaken housekeeping prompt
- Old finished chats you do not need in the active sidebar

**Do not archive yet**
- Anything showing `Apply`, `Open`, or `Create PR`
- Anything with a running spinner, stop button, or in-progress command
- Anything that mentions a commit, diff, branch, or PR you have not reviewed
- Anything you still need to act on

**Fast visual test**
- `Apply / Open / Create PR` visible: not safe yet
- Spinner or active run visible: not safe yet
- Empty duplicate housekeeping thread: safe
- Normal finished chat with no controls and no pending action: safe

**Important safety valve**
Archiving is much safer than prompting, because archived threads can be restored later. It hides them from the active sidebar; it does not mean “permanently gone.” Docs: [thread archive/unarchive](https://developers.openai.com/codex/app-server/#api-overview), [worktree cleanup](https://developers.openai.com/codex/app/worktrees/#worktree-cleanup)

So the practical answer is:
- Archive duplicate housekeeping threads aggressively.
- Review cloud/result threads conservatively.
- Never use a prompt to decide whether to archive.

If you want, I can give you a thread-by-thread verdict from your sidebar using only the names and visible controls: `archive now`, `review first`, or `leave open`.

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: SHERMAN PROTOCOLS ENGAGED ; "MARCH TO THE SEA"

payload.message: SHERMAN PROTOCOLS ENGAGED ; "MARCH TO THE SEA"

If that means “purge the sidebar hard,” here is the aggressive but safe version:

1. Keep exactly one anchor thread open:
   - `Awaken Codex alert`

2. Archive immediately, without opening for chat:
   - every `Verify...`
   - every `Clarify...`
   - every `Handle...`
   - any obviously accidental duplicate thread

3. For each `Linear Mention...` or cloud-result thread:
   - If it shows `Apply`, `Open`, or `Create PR`, do not prompt it.
   - Decide outside the thread whether you care about its output.
   - If you do not care, close any unwanted PR in GitHub first, then archive the thread.
   - If you do care, keep only that one open for review.

4. Do not type into old threads at all.
   - No `archive this thread`
   - No `verify completion`
   - No cleanup prompts of any kind

5. End state:
   - `Awaken Codex alert`
   - plus at most one actively reviewed `Linear Mention...` thread
   - everything else archived

This is the key safety fact: archive is reversible. If you over-prune, you can unarchive later. So for junk duplicates, bias toward archiving.

If you want, send one more screenshot after the first purge pass and I’ll mark the survivors `keep` vs `archive`.

content.text: BLIND ARCHIVED: ALL

payload.message: BLIND ARCHIVED: ALL

That was actually the right move.

Archived is recoverable, and it’s safer than continuing to let Codex spawn more side effects from stale threads. You’ve reset the active surface, which was the real goal.

What to do next:
- Open only the one thread you want to keep working in.
- Do not resurrect old housekeeping threads.
- If you need something from an archived thread, restore only that single thread.
- If unwanted PRs were created during the chaos, clean those up in GitHub separately.

Going forward, use this operating rule:
- active sidebar = only live work
- archive via UI, never via prompt
- one coordination thread at a time whenever possible

`CODEX COMPLETE: work finished, no further action pending in this thread. Ready for termination or archive.`

Codex project config for this lives in `.codex/config.toml`. VS Code agent-mode
config lives in `.vscode/mcp.json`.

---

## Machine-Readable Registry

See `swarm.json` at repo root for the structured machine-readable registry (agents, personas, systems, protocols).

---

## Governance

All agents are governed by `!/CONSTITUTION.md`. All agents share conventions in `!/VAULT-CONVENTIONS.md`. Logan is human; agents are software. Logan directs; agents execute.

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

payload.message: NEXT?

Next is to rebuild a clean single-thread workflow.

1. Start one fresh Codex thread.
2. Use this as the opening prompt:

```text
IDAHO-VAULT. Work only in this thread unless I explicitly ask otherwise. Do not create new threads, PRs, or housekeeping actions unless I explicitly request them. Use status signals only:
- CODEX ACTIVE
- CODEX PAUSED: awaiting Logan
- CODEX COMPLETE: work finished, no further action pending in this thread. Ready for termination or archive.
```

3. In GitHub, close any junk PRs that were created during the thread-multiplicity mess.
4. Keep the sidebar empty except for the one thread you are actively using.
5. Archive by UI only, never by prompt.

If you want the cleanest possible restart, the very next thread should be either:
- `Codex control thread`
or
- the one real task you want done next

If you want, I can draft the exact first prompt for that new control thread.
